texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of amine absorption technologies developed for iron works is evaluated for two base cases, con V entional blast furnace top gas and nitrogen free blast furnace top gas with shaft injection. The study includes well-known absorbents like monoethanolamine (MEA) and methyldiethanolamine (MDEA)/piperazine, as well as 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP). A rate-based closed-loop model was developed for AMP based on a previously developed rigorous model. For the AMP model, equilibrium data have been obtained in our own laboratories. Simulations show that it is very favorable with intercooling of the absorber. Inserting a precooler at the gas inlet to the absorber does not cause significant operational cost improvements. For higher piperazine activator concentrations, up to 20 wt % Pz, lower regeneration duties as well as lower circulation rates are achieved. A comparison between the amines shows that the AMP is most energy efficient, followed by MDEA/Pz and MEA. By combining the best amine with process intercooling, a heat requirement of only 2.2 MJ/(ton of CO2 recovered) is possible for treatment of conventional blast furnace top gas.

texts.orig: 1.2. Investment and Operating Costs of an Amine Based CO2 Removal System. The main investment items for the absorption system are the two towers, the inlet cooler, and the lean/rich crossflow heat exchanger. The main operating costs are connected to the consumption of steam and electricity. In terms of electricity, which amounts to about 10 -20% of the total energy required, the largest consumers are the product CO2 compressors, the inlet flue gas blower, and, to a smaller extent, the two pumps for amine circulation.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the past decade, there has been great focus on greenhouse gas emissions from the power industry. The possibility to capture and store CO2 into geological reservoirs has triggered the interest to explore possible schemes for capturing CO2, including precombustion, oxy-fuel, and postcombustion routes. Large research programs have been initiated all over the word. In most comparative studies, the postcombustion route, where the exhaust gas from coal or gas-fired gas plants is captured at atmospheric pressures, has been found to be the most inexpensive alternative (at least in a near-term perspective). One example is the recent published report from the Carbon Capture Project, 1 which was performed by eight of the world's largest energy companies. In addition, this report concluded that postcombustion capture with chemical absorption also has a substantial potential for improvement. These improvements may be achieved by use of more suitable amine mixtures, by better energy optimization of the absorber/desorber process, and by improved thermal integration with the power plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to optimize the absorber/desorber process and assess the applicability of the process for the different ULCOS cases, we have applied two different simulation programs. These programs calculate the different stream compositions in the process, the necessary steam to the reboiler, the packing height in the towers, the heat exchanger areas, etc. The parameters from these calculations are the basis for the economic evaluations. The commercial simulator ProTreat version 2.1 2 was used for the simulations covering the amines monoethanolamine (MEA) and methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) activated with piperazine. The simulations concerning adenosine 2-amino-2methyl-1-propanol (AMP) were conducted with our own simulation model 3,4 after modification for the use of AMP and validation against pilot-plant data. A cross-comparison of the two simulators was performed using MEA.

A cross-comparison of the two simulators was performed using MEA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.1. Solvents Used in the Simulations. Three main classes of amines are generally used as chemical solvents. These are primary, secondary, and tertiary amines. The primary amines, such as MEA, react strongly and quickly and work at low CO2 partial pressures. The reactivity of the amines decreases as more substituents are added to form secondary and tertiary amines, such as DEA or MDEA, respectively. MDEA, as a tertiary amine, has, in general, much lower regeneration costs compared to primary and secondary amines. This, in addition to other properties such as lower corrosion rates and slower degradation rates compared to MEA, has made this amine attractive for natural gas CO2 cleaning. It could be noted that, unlike MEA, MDEAwill not work properly at low CO2 partial pressures (e.g., for flue gases) because the rate of reaction is too slow, maybe even with a promoter , and because of the low capacity at low partial pressures. However, the given blast furnace gases in Cases A and B (see Table 1 for definititions) have a CO2 inlet partial pressure of 100 kPa and may possibly benefit from MDEA based solutions. The last amine tested, AMP, is a sterically hindered primary amine, which, because of the molecular structure, has a much lower reaction rate and reacts more like tertiary amines (does not form carbamates). In this work, three different amine systems have been tested in the simulations as given in Table 2.

texts.orig: 2.4. Process Configurations. 2.4.1. Intercooling. The absorber was modeled as two separate packed columns in order to simulate the intercooled solvent. After exiting the upper column, the partially loaded amine is cooled in a heat exchanger and added to a second column representing the lower part of the absorber. This can be especially favorable because of the large temperature increase of the amine solution in the column due to the exothermic absorption of the large amounts of CO2 to be removed in the steel work cases.

This can be especially favorable because of the large temperature increase of the amine solution in the column due to the exothermic absorption of the large amounts of CO2 to be removed in the steel work cases.

texts.orig: 2.4.2. Gas Compression Prior to or After the Absorber. In case B, the pressure specification of the cleaned gas is 3 bar, and in order to meet this specification, one must compress the gas either before or after the absorber. Simulations were run to compare these two configurations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.4.3. Direct Contact Cooling of Inlet Gas Stream. A case that included a cooler on the inlet gas was simulated to see the effects of cooling the inlet gas as well as removing some of the water content. Both the temperature and the water content of the inlet gas will affect the absorption reaction. The exothermic process is, in general, favored by lower temperature, by making it possible to obtain a higher loading at the bottom of the absorber. However, too low a temperature will adversely slow down the kinetics. An optimum should, therefore, be found.

texts.orig: 3.1.1. Monoethanolamine (MEA) 30 wt %. Three different configurations were tested as shown in the first three columns in Table 3 (explanations of the configurations are given in the footnote below the table). Each of the configurations using MEA was optimized according to the procedure described in Section 2.3, and an optimum run for each configuration is indicated by MEA 1 -MEA 3 in Table 3. With the basic configuration MEA 1, corresponding to the flowsheet shown in Figure 2, an optimized case will require a reboiler steam consumption of 3.18 MJ/kg of CO2 and a circulation rate of 15.7 (m 3 of solution)/(ton of CO2 removed). This assumes a 96% approach to equilibrium in the absorber bottom with a total packing section of 6 m and gives a lean loading of 0.26 (mol of CO2)/ (mol of amine).

texts.orig: As shown by the case MEA 2, the inlet cooler does not give a significant reduction in steam consumption. On the other hand, intercooling of the absorber is very favorable as can be seen by a comparison of MEA 1 and MEA 3. The improved performance is due to the increased absorption capacity at the lower outlet amine solution temperature. Regarding carbon monoxide (CO) content, the absorbent uptake in the absorber is 33 kg/h. In the following flash drum after absorption, a pressure reduction from 2.53 to 2.1 bar will lead to about 20% release of the absorbed CO and the purified CO2 product will contain 170 ppm of CO. This is an acceptable value for geological storage and for enhanced oil recovery (EOR), but for CO2 transport by ship, an additional flash drum should be installed to reduce the content down to < 5 ppm.

texts.orig: 3.1.2. MDEA/Piperazine 50 wt %. In Table 3 are also shown simulation results for 35 wt % MDEA solvent activated with 15 wt % piperazine (Pz) where four process configurations have been tested. From the table, it can be seen that intercooling in the absorber is favorable also for the MDEA/Pz system. Comparing configuration MDEA/Pz 1 and MDEA/Pz 3 shows that significantly more steam is required without an intercooled absorber. In MDEA/Pz 3, < 80% of the CO2 is removed and the reboiler temperature is increased. Inserting a precooler at the gas inlet to the absorber does not cause significant operational cost improvements, as shown by case MDEA/Pz 2. It can also be noted that using more cooling water for intercooling does not lead to significant savings, as shown by comparing MDEA/Pz 1 and MDEA/Pz 4, where the liquid solution temperature is reduced from 40 to 30 C. This indicates an optimum around 40 C as slower kinetics become significant at the lower temperatures. The definition for CO2 loading for the MDEA/Pz blend is (mol of CO2)/(mol of total amine). It can be noted that the approaches to equilibrium for the MEA cases run very high; thus, an improvement of the energy efficiency for the cases with an increase in packing height from 6 to 10 m will be small, making the MEA/MDEA comparison valid.

texts.orig: From Table 5, it can be seen that the simulation results with the present model are quite similar to those of the ProTreat simulations. When comparing the two simulators using the same packing height, the ProTreat simulations show higher reboiler duties as well as a lower absorption percentage. This is to be expected because two different packing materials have been used. In comparing MEA 3_1 and MEA 3_3, we may notice that the cyclic capacity, i.e., the difference between the rich and lean loadings, is found to be larger using CO2Sim. The main explanation is also the difference in packing material and the correlations for effective interfacial gas/liquid area used in the two different programs. Until a more thorough investigation of the accuracy of these different correlations has been performed, one cannot say which simulator gives the more correct description of the packing performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Table 5, the MEA 3_2 simulation is also compared with simulations with various absorber packing heights and reboiler duties using AMP. As can be seen from AMP 3_1, the rate of absorption with AMP is too slow to keep the same absorber size as for MEA 3_2 . A significant increase in packing height is necessary. Conversely, the desorber packing height can be reduced. By increasing the absorber size, it is possible to utilize a larger loading range with the benefit of lower regeneration costs. This results in a lower reboiler temperature and significantly reduced steam consumption as the absorber size increases. Because of the lower regeneration cost compared to MEA, the size of the desorber can be drastically reduced. In fact, at these conditions, the desorber does not contribute much at all to the regeneration of the amine. The desorber mainly has the function of providing latent and sensible heat to the liquid stream and heating it up. Hardly any stripping occurs in the desorber column. The stripping occurs in the reboiler, and the desorber packing height can be minimized. It can be noted that the stripper cannot be omitted completely since it is necessary to have good heat exchange of the rich feed to bring it to the stripping temperature. The pressure drop has been assumed constant at 1kPa, any implications of varying pressure drop are not shown in the simulations. A typical pressure drop of 0.2kPa/m of packing will imply a drop of 0.6 -6 kPa in the different cases, and the effect of this is deemed insignificant.

texts.orig: 3.6. Influence of Accuracy of Reaction Rates and Correlations for Effective Interfacial Area. In rate-based model approaches, as used in CO2Sim and in ProTreat, the absorber packing height will be determined, to a large extent, by the reaction kinetics, the transport properties of the system, and the effective interfacial area of the packing. The accuracy of the calculated effective interfacial area for the absorption and desorption process relies on the correlations for the hydraulics in the column determining the effective contact area. In general, the correlations for estimating interfacial areas for absorption processes vary significantly in the literature, and the accuracy of these correlations is questionable. The kinetic data for AMP that are found in the literature also vary a lot, as noted in Appendix 1.
texts.text: 3.6. Influence of Accuracy of Reaction Rates and Correlations for Effective Interfacial Area. In rate-based model approaches, as used in CO2Sim and in ProTreat, the absorber packing height will be determined, to a large extent, by the reaction kinetics, the transport properties of the system, and the effective interfacial area of the packing. The accuracy of the calculated effective interfacial area for the absorption and desorption process relies on the correlations for the hydraulics in the column determining the effective contact area. In general, the correlations for estimating interfacial areas for absorption processes vary significantly in the literature, and the accuracy of these correlations is questionable. The kinetic data for AMP that are found in the literature also vary a lot, as noted in Appendix 1.

texts.orig: 3.6.1.1. Estimated Energy Requirement Comparison with Intercooled Absorber. Because the effect of intercooling is large, we have compared the different amines for this configuration, too. In simulating the effect of intercooling on operations with AMP, it is assumed that the same gain as found for MEAand MDEA/Pz can be obtained, that is, 17% gain in steam consumption, 6% increase in CO2 recovery, and a 6% reduction in the circulation rates. As explained earlier, AMP will benefit from operation with intercooler, and the 17% reduction in reboiler energy must be regarded as a conservative estimate.

texts.orig: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From the simulations of the cases, two lists of conclusions are given, one for the process configuration part and one for the amine solvent part of the study.
texts.text: From the simulations of the cases, two lists of conclusions are given, one for the process configuration part and one for the amine solvent part of the study.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 4.1. Process Configurations. Although it has not been possible to simulate all the different configurations with all the amines, some conclusions may be drawn:
texts.text: 4.1. Process Configurations. Although it has not been possible to simulate all the different configurations with all the amines, some conclusions may be drawn:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (1) It is very favorable with intercooling in the absorber. For the studied Case A, the thermal heat requirement was reduced by 17 -18% for both MEA and MDEA/Pz. In addition, the CO2 recovery increased from 80 to 84% for MEA and from 78 to 86% for MDEA/Pz. The improved performance is due to the increased absorption capacity in the lower part of the column.
texts.text: (1) It is very favorable with intercooling in the absorber. For the studied Case A, the thermal heat requirement was reduced by 17 -18% for both MEA and MDEA/Pz. In addition, the CO2 recovery increased from 80 to 84% for MEA and from 78 to 86% for MDEA/Pz. The improved performance is due to the increased absorption capacity in the lower part of the column.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (2) Inserting a precooler at the gas inlet to the absorber does not cause significant operational cost improvements.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Comparison of the Different Solvents.
texts.text: 4.2. Comparison of the Different Solvents.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) The lowest circulation rates are accomplished using MEA, although at the cost of significant higher steam requirements compared to those of the MDEA/Pz blend.
texts.text: The lowest circulation rates are accomplished using MEA, although at the cost of significant higher steam requirements compared to those of the MDEA/Pz blend.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) The studies show that the MDEA/Pz blend is more efficient than MEA in terms of reducing the reboiler duty. For higher piperazine activator concentrations, up to 20 wt % Pz, lower regeneration duties as well as lower circulation rates are achieved.
texts.text: The studies show that the MDEA/Pz blend is more efficient than MEA in terms of reducing the reboiler duty. For higher piperazine activator concentrations, up to 20 wt % Pz, lower regeneration duties as well as lower circulation rates are achieved.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (3) The simulations show that AMP can be even more favorable as an alternative to the other amines tested. Its slow absorption rate requires a large absorber, but the desorber can be significantly smaller. An addition of activator should be investigated to increase the absorption rate and, thus, reduce the absorber size. The comparison between all the amines shows that AMP is the most energy-efficient amine (based on steam consumption), compared with MDEA/Pz and MEA.

The comparison between all the amines shows that AMP is the most energy-efficient amine (based on steam consumption), compared with MDEA/Pz and MEA.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (4) By combining the best amine with intercooling, a consumption of only 2.2 MJ/ton of CO2 seems to be possible.

texts.orig: For the simulations with AMP, the following changes were made.
texts.text: For the simulations with AMP, the following changes were made.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Vapor -Liquid Equilibrium (VLE). The equilibrium model for AMP was developed with a simplified VLE model because of a lack of a full set of accurate physiochemical and mass transfer data for the amine. The model was fitted to VLE data, obtained in our lab for 3.0 kmol/m 3 AMP solution, together with the data of Park et al. (2002), 7 as an explicit function dependent on CO2 loading and temperature. The model fit is shown in Figure A1. By using such a VLE model, the interface mass transfer models were also modified from the developed rigorous model by application of an enhancement-factor model for the absorber and desorber. When MEA was used as solvent, the same simplifying modifications were made and thereby compared with the results from ProTreat as a way of validating the explicit model for the set of amines and to form the basis for comparison in the simulation study. The simplified MEA model was also compared with the rigorous model earlier presented. 3
